techniques.evidence_quote: It seems the adversaries who published this AMI designed it to bill AWS customers for compute while extracting cryptocurrency.

techniques.technique_name: Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
techniques.evidence_quote: Experts were doing an incident investigation for a financial institution when they needed to look at some Windows 2008 Server machines.
Mitiga estimates this AMI has been around for five years and the cryptominer was running in it from the beginning.
it was using way more compute resources than it was supposed to use... the mined cryptocurrency goes to the attacker.

attack_chain.description: Attacker created a Windows Server 2008 Community AMI and embedded a Monero cryptominer within it before publishing it publicly on AWS.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: It seems the adversaries who published this AMI designed it to bill AWS customers for compute while extracting cryptocurrency.

attack_chain.technique_name: Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
attack_chain.description: Victim organization (a financial institution) launched EC2 instances using the trojanized Community AMI, inheriting the embedded malware as part of their infrastructure.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Experts were doing an incident investigation for a financial institution when they needed to look at some Windows 2008 Server machines.

attack_chain.description: The malicious AMI persisted across every new EC2 instance launch derived from it, ensuring the cryptominer was active from instance boot without further attacker interaction, for an estimated five years.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Mitiga estimates this AMI has been around for five years and the cryptominer was running in it from the beginning.

attack_chain.description: The embedded Monero cryptominer ran on victim-funded EC2 compute resources, consuming excess CPU/compute while directing mined cryptocurrency to the attacker's wallet.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: it was using way more compute resources than it was supposed to use... the mined cryptocurrency goes to the attacker.

initial_access_vector: Victim launched an EC2 instance from a trojanized Community AMI (Windows Server 2008) that had a Monero cryptominer pre-embedded by the attacker at publish time.
persistence_mechanism: Malware was baked directly into the Community AMI image (T1525 - Implant Internal Image), ensuring it executed on every instance launched from the AMI without requiring ongoing attacker access.
